headline: Survey at 23% target with strong teacher engagement but uneven regional coverage.
collection_status: 305 responses collected against 1,350 target puts you at pace for ~914 total by end of cycle—22.6% shortfall. Lindi, Mara, and Tanga account for 58% of responses; Rukwa and Manyara critically underrepresented with only 9 combined responses.
key_findings: Teacher cohort dominates (150/305; 49%)—lower primary KiuFunza teachers drive bulk participation. Head teachers (74) provide institutional perspective but non-KiuFunza teachers (2) and regional officials (3) underrepresented.

Expand KiuFunza (112) and launch new programs (64) are overwhelming priorities (58% of all responses), signalling appetite for scaling. Policy integration (60) ranks third, indicating stakeholders see sustainability risk.
Partner satisfaction strong at 4.4/5 but scale readiness weak at 2.8/5—gap suggests confidence in current work doesn't translate to confidence in expansion capacity.
Feedback themes: incentivise teachers, expand to new schools/districts, formalise KiuFunza in education policy, digitise monitoring. Limited evidence of systemic barriers or resistance.
flags: Regional coverage collapse: Rukwa (5) and Manyara (4) at 1-2% of target. Immediate field audit required to diagnose access, stakeholder mapping, or implementation gaps.
Scale readiness (2.8/5) contradicts expansion appetite (112 votes). Without operationalised readiness plan, scaling risks quality dilution and partner burnout.
Respondent type skew: 74% are teachers/head teachers; district/regional officials represent <4%. Missing perspectives from budget holders and policy gatekeepers needed to unlock policy priority.
recommendation: Redirect 40% of collection effort to Rukwa and Manyara immediately—conduct rapid stakeholder stocktake and barrier analysis. In parallel, commission focused scale readiness assessment with 30-40 respondents across all regions to convert the policy momentum into actionable capacity roadmap before next quarter.
